name: Lapurdian Basque (Lapurtera)

ancestors.notes: Standard Basque base; Lapurdian is the dialect closest to classical written Basque

notes: Lapurdian Basque (Lapurtera / Labourdian). Spoken in Labourd/Lapurdi, French Basque Country (around Bayonne, Biarritz, Saint-Jean-de-Luz). In Zuazo's classification Lapurdian and Lower Navarrese form the single Navarro-Lapurdian dialect. Classical literary dialect: Axular's Gero (1643), written in the speech of Sara (Lapurdi), is the canonical Basque prose text. VALIDATED against Hualde (2018), 'Aspiration in Basque'. KEY FEATURES: (1) ASPIRATION — RECEDING: classical 17th-century Lapurdian preserved both the laryngeal fricative /h/ and aspirated stops [pʰ tʰ kʰ], amply attested in Axular's Gero (Hualde 2018: 5, list (1)); however, in the coastal French Basque Country aspiration was already lost by the 19th century (Hualde 2018: 2, citing Michelena FHV VI:168). Modern coastal Lapurdian therefore has /h/ only variably/residually — modelled here as h -> [h] ~ [∅]. Unlike Souletin, modern Lapurdian has no productive aspirated-stop series. (2) FULL SIBILANT CONTRAST: /s̺ s̻ ʃ/ and /ts̺ ts̻ tʃ/ are maintained as in the conservative base. (3) PALATAL STOPS /c ɟ/ maintained. (4) FRENCH CONTACT: uvular [ʁ] for /r/ and /ɾ/ appears in French-contact speech alongside the inherited alveolar [r]/[ɾ] (contact feature; no dedicated page cite). (5) Conservative morphology, close to the classical literary norm.

sources.author: Hualde, J.I.
sources.title: Aspiration in Basque
sources.notes: Primary read source: aspirated stops and /h/ in Axular's 17th-c. Lapurdian (p.5, list (1)); 19th-c. loss of aspiration in coastal French Basque (p.84 fn.).

sources.author: Hualde, J.I. & Ortiz de Urbina, J. (eds.)
sources.title: A Grammar of Basque

sources.author: Oyharçabal, B.
sources.title: The Dialects of the Basque Language
sources.publisher: In: A Grammar of Basque, Mouton de Gruyter
